title: Supraventricular Tachycardia
oneLiner: A narrow‑complex tachycardia (≥150 bpm) originating above the ventricles, common in young adults, that usually terminates with vagal manoeuvres or adenosine – misdiagnosis or missing pre‑excited AF can be dangerous.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:** A 28‑year‑old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden‑onset palpitations, light‑headedness, and a sense of impending doom. She has had similar episodes since her teens, each starting and ending abruptly. She is anxious and tachypnoeic, BP 105/70, heart rate 190 bpm, lungs clear. Her ECG shows a narrow‑complex tachycardia (QRS <120 ms) with no visible P waves. After a 6 mg IV adenosine push, the rhythm briefly shows a sinus pause with a subtle P‑wave change, then restores to sinus rhythm.
- **Dissect the vignette:**
  - *Sudden onset & offset* → typical for AV nodal re‑entrant tachycardia (AVNRT) or AV re‑entrant tachycardia (AVRT, Wolff‑Parkinson‑White). Atrial fibrillation/flutter often starts more gradually.
  - *Young adult + female* → AVNRT is more common in women; WPW often presents in childhood/young adulthood.
  - *Sense of impending doom + light‑headedness* → reduced cerebral perfusion due to high rate and short diastole; patient may also have polyuria (ANP release).
  - *Narrow QRS* → supraventricular origin; rate >150 rules out sinus tachycardia in most cases.
  - *No visible P waves* → in AVNRT, atria and ventricles are activated simultaneously so P waves are buried in the QRS; in AVRT, P waves may be seen after the QRS (retrograde P).
  - *Adenosine response* → termination of tachycardia + brief sinus pause is diagnostic of AVNRT/AVRT (adenosine‑sensitive circuit). Atrial flutter will not terminate – it may slow transiently.
- **Atypical presentations:**
  - **Wide QRS SVT (aberrancy):** Pre‑existing bundle branch block or rate‑dependent aberrancy mimics ventricular tachycardia (VT). Clues: history of SVT, adenosine termination, concordance of QRS morphology with known baseline BBB.
  - **Pre‑excited atrial fibrillation (WPW + AF):** Very fast irregular wide‑complex tachycardia (may exceed 250–300 bpm) – no adenosine! Risk of degeneration to VF. Look for delta waves in sinus rhythm.
  - **SVT in elderly:** May present with syncope, falls, or heart failure (rate‑related cardiomyopathy) without palpitations. ECG may show atrial flutter with variable block.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) is any tachyarrhythmia originating above the bifurcation of the His bundle, with a narrow QRS (<120 ms) unless conduction is aberrant. The most common types are AVNRT (~60%), AVRT (~30%), and atrial tachycardia (~10%).
- It matters because SVT affects 2.25 per 1000 people, often causing debilitating symptoms and emergency visits. Most patients have structurally normal hearts, so management is safe and curative. Pitfalls: confusing SVT with VT (leads to wrong treatment), giving adenosine in pre‑excited AF (risk of converting to VF), or missing tachyarrhythmia‑induced cardiomyopathy.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **The circuit story:** In AVNRT, there are two functional pathways inside the AV node – a "slow" pathway with a short refractory period and a "fast" pathway with a long refractory period. A premature atrial contraction (PAC) blocks in the fast pathway (still refractory) but conducts down the slow pathway. By the time the impulse reaches the AV node exit, the fast pathway has recovered, allowing the impulse to travel back up (retrograde) – creating a re‑entry loop. The atria and ventricles are activated nearly simultaneously, so P waves are hidden.
- **Why the heart rate is rapid:** The re‑entrant circuit has a short cycle length (typically 160–250 ms), so the ventricles are captured at every beat (1:1 conduction) giving rates of 150–250 bpm.
- **Why patients feel light‑headed:** High rate reduces diastolic filling time → decreased stroke volume → drop in cerebral perfusion. The sudden onset also triggers a catecholamine surge (anxiety, pallor).
- **Why vagal manoeuvres/adenosine work:** They increase AV nodal refractoriness, interrupting the re‑entrant loop. If the circuit is not AV node‑dependent (e.g., atrial tachycardia) they only slow the ventricular rate but don't terminate it.
- **Why the ECG shows no P waves (AVNRT):** Atrial activation is retrograde and simultaneous with ventricular activation – P waves are buried in the QRS or appear as “pseudo‑r’ in V1. In typical AVRT (WPW), the circuit goes down the AV node and up the accessory pathway, so the atria activate slightly after the ventricles → P waves seen just after the QRS (retrograde P, often inverted in II/III/aVF).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | AVNRT (no structural heart disease) | Young, female, recurrent episodes since teens |
| **Congenital** | Accessory pathway (WPW) | Delta waves on sinus ECG, sudden onset in children |
| **Reversible triggers** | Caffeine, alcohol, stress, thyroid storm, electrolyte abnormalities (hypomagnesemia, hypokalemia) | Patient reports heavy coffee intake or exam stress |
| **Drug‑induced** | Sympathomimetics, theophylline, digoxin toxicity (atrial tachycardia with block) | Patient on inhalers or heart failure meds |
| **Cardiac pathology** | Valvular disease, cardiomyopathy, pulmonary embolism (atrial flutter/fibrillation) | Dyspnoea, raised JVP, murmur, acute hypoxia |
| **Post‑operative** | Cardiac surgery (atrial incisions/scars → atrial flutter/tachycardia) | Recent heart surgery, ICU patient |

- **Most common cause overall:** AV nodal re‑entrant tachycardia (AVNRT) in the absence of structural disease.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
- **12‑lead ECG during tachycardia – gold standard:**
  - *What to look for:* Narrow QRS (<120 ms), rate >150, regularity, P‑wave morphology/location.
  - *Confirmation of SVT:* Terminates with adenosine (diagnostic for AVNRT/AVRT).
  - *Pattern recognition:* If visible P waves are inverted in II/III/aVF and appear before the QRS → atrial tachycardia (rate usually 100–250 bpm). If they appear after the QRS (ST‑segment) → AVRT (retrograde P).
  - *Single finding that confirms AVNRT:* No visible P waves + sudden onset/offset + response to adenosine.
  - *What rules out VT:* QRS duration ≤120 ms; if wide, look for dissociated P waves or fusion beats (VT). In pre‑excited AF, wide QRS but irregularly irregular – cannot give adenosine.
- **ECG in sinus rhythm (baseline):**
  - Look for delta waves (WPW) or short PR interval (Lown‑Ganong‑Levine syndrome).
  - Normal ECG does not rule out AVNRT.
- **Echocardiogram:**
  - *Why:* To exclude structural heart disease, assess LV function (rate‑related cardiomyopathy).
  - *When:* All first‑presentation SVT, especially if response to adenosine is atypical or history of syncope.
- **Holter monitor (24–48 h) or event recorder:**
  - *Why:* Capture intermittent episodes not recorded on 12‑lead.
  - *Interpretation:* “Sudden onset narrow complex tachycardia” confirms SVT.
- **Laboratory:**
  - *What:* FBC, U&E, Mg, Ca, TSH.
  - *Why:* Rule out reversible causes; high TSH low T4 suggests thyrotoxicosis; K <3.5 or Mg <0.7 augments re‑entry.
- **Electrophysiology study (EPS):**
  - *Why:* Definitive diagnosis + catheter ablation.
  - *When:* Recurrent symptomatic SVT, failed medical therapy, WPW with pre‑excited AF, high‑risk occupations (pilots, athletes).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
- **Immediate (first hour) – assume haemodynamically stable:**
  1. **Vagal manoeuvres** – Valsalva or carotid sinus massage (in absence of carotid bruit). *Why:* Increases vagal tone, blocks AV node, terminates re‑entrant circuit. Success rate ~30–50%.
  2. **Adenosine** – 6 mg rapid IV push (≥2 second) followed by 10 mL saline flush, then if no effect at 1–2 min give 12 mg, max 12 mg.
     - *Why:* Blocks AV nodal conduction – terminates AVNRT/AVRT.
     - *What to watch:* Transient asystole, flushing, bronchospasm (avoid in asthma – use verapamil instead).
     - *Pitfall:* If rhythm is atrial flutter/tachycardia, you'll see transient slowing but no termination; do not repeat unnecessarily.
  3. **If failed:** Synchronised cardioversion (50–100 J biphasic) if unstable (hypotension, chest pain, altered mental state – rare in pure SVT). *Why:* Direct current interrupts re‑entry.
- **Ward (24–72 h) – after first episode:**
  - **Monitor rhythm** (telemetry for 24 h) to detect recurrence.
  - **Start long‑term rate control** if recurrence is likely or patient wishes:
    - First‑line (AVNRT/AVRT): β‑blocker (e.g., metoprolol 25–50 mg PO BD) or verapamil 40–120 mg PO TDS.
    - *Why:* Slows AV nodal conduction and reduces triggers.
  - **WPW patients:** Risk of pre‑excited AF – avoid verapamil/digoxin (may enhance accessory pathway conduction). Flecainide 50–150 mg PO BD is preferred (class IC) – but contraindicated in structural heart disease.
  - **Patient education:** Advise to avoid triggers (caffeine, alcohol), demonstrate Valsalva, and return if palpitations recur.
- **Long‑term:**
  - **Catheter ablation** – definitive curative procedure (success >95% for AVNRT/AVRT). *Why:* Eliminates the re‑entrant circuit.
  - **When:** Recurrent SVT despite medication, WPW with high risk (pre‑excited AF, syncope), drug intolerance, or patient preference.
  - **After ablation:** Recurrence ~5%; lifelong follow‑up with ECG if WPW ablated (delta wave may persist).
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do not use adenosine in wide‑complex tachycardia of uncertain origin (may cause VF if VT).
  - Do not give verapamil/digoxin in WPW + AF – they slow AV node conduction but can accelerate conduction through accessory pathway, risking VF.
  - Do not delay cardioversion in unstable patient – adenosine is not safe when BP is low.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | SVT (AVNRT/AVRT) | Atrial Flutter | Atrial Fibrillation | Ventricular Tachycardia |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| **Rate** | 150–250 | 150 (if 2:1) or 300 (1:1) | 100–180 (uncontrolled) | 100–250 |
| **Rhythm** | Regular | Regular (sawtooth F waves) | Irregularly irregular | Usually regular |
| **Onset/Offset** | Abrupt | Can be abrupt or gradual | Often gradual | Usually abrupt |
| **QRS width** | Narrow (<120 ms) | Narrow (unless wide with block) | Narrow (unless pre‑excited) | Wide (>120 ms) |
| **P waves** | Hidden (AVNRT) or after QRS (AVRT) | Flutter waves (sawtooth in II/III/aVF) | Fibrillatory waves, no P | Dissociated P waves (AV dissociation) |
| **Response to adenosine** | Termination | No termination – transient slowing | No termination – transient slowing | No change (rarely termination) |
| **Key differentiator** | Adenosine terminates | Flutter waves + carotid massage slows rate | Irregular R‑R intervals | AV dissociation, fusion beats |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Tachyarrhythmia‑induced cardiomyopathy:** Chronic uncontrolled SVT (rates >130 for months) can cause reversible left ventricular systolic dysfunction. *Presentation:* Exertional dyspnoea, fatigue, signs of heart failure. *Key signs:* Tachycardia on ECG, low LVEF on echo. *What to do:* Rate control or ablation → LVEF often normalises over 3–6 months.
- **Pre‑excited AF → VF:** Missed WPW + AF → extremely fast ventricular response (250+ bpm) can degenerate into VF/sudden cardiac death. *Presentation:* Collapse, wide irregular tachycardia. *Management:* Immediate DC cardioversion (200 J). *Pitfall:* Do not give AV nodal blockers (adenosine, verapamil, beta blockers) – they may increase accessory pathway conduction.
- **Syncope/falls:** Severe cerebral hypoperfusion during prolonged episode → injury. *Late presentation:* Elderly hip fracture from fall during palpitations.
- **Cardiogenic shock (rare):** Prolonged high‑rate SVT with structural heart disease → pulmonary oedema. *Signs:* Raised JVP, basal crackles, hypotension. *Action:* Urgent synchronised cardioversion.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonic for SVT causes in the young:** “AVN” (AVNRT, AVRT – WPW, Atrial Tachycardia).
- **Mnemonic for distinguishing SVT from VT:** "Brugada criteria" – but simpler: **HAS** (Haemodynamics, Adenosine response, Scar?) – if haemodynamically unstable → assume VT until proven. If adenosine terminates → SVT.
- **Pearls:**
  1. **Always get a 12‑lead during tachycardia** – not just in sinus rhythm.
  2. **If you see a wide‑complex tachycardia and the rhythm is irregular, think pre‑excited AF first** – do NOT give adenosine.
  3. **Valsalva is underused** – instruct patient to blow into a 10 mL syringe (to pop the plunger out) while supine; increased success.
  4. **Adenosine has a diagnostic window** – even if it doesn't terminate, transient slowing reveals underlying atrial activity (flutter waves).
  5. **SVT that starts gradually (warm‑up) is more likely atrial tachycardia** – re‑entrant circuits start and end abruptly.
  6. **Children with WPW and syncope** – risk stratification: if pre‑excitation persists during exercise stress test, ablation is indicated.
  7. **Never discharge a patient with SVT without a baseline ECG** – if delta waves are present, avoid verapamil.
- **Common exam/ward mistakes:**
  - Calling tachycardia “sinus” without checking rate >150 (sinus rarely >150 in adults).
  - Giving adenosine in AF with WPW.
  - Mistaking atrial flutter with 2:1 block for sinus tachycardia – the flutter waves are often hidden; check for sawtooth in II, III, aVF.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU team for:**
  - Haemodynamic instability: SBP <90 mmHg, altered mental state, chest pain, acute pulmonary oedema.
  - Wide‑complex tachycardia of uncertain origin (especially irregular) – needs cardiologist input urgently.
  - SVT refractory to adenosine (two doses) with ongoing symptoms – consider elective cardioversion.
  - Pre‑excited AF (WPW) – high risk of VF; manage in a monitored area with defibrillator nearby.
  - Recurrent SVT despite medical therapy in a patient with structural heart disease (low EF) – risk of decompensation.
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥7** (any single parameter red: RR >25, SpO2 <91%, SBP <91, HR >131) → escalate immediately.
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (indicating tissue hypoperfusion) → consider cardioversion.
  - **GCS ≤13** (especially if decreased from baseline) → protect airway, call for anaesthetic support.
  - **Sustained rate >250 bpm** → risk of degeneration; prepare defibrillator.
  - **Ventricular rate >200 bpm in AF** → risk of pre‑excitation; cardiology review.

*Final thought:* In any tachyarrhythmia, take a step back: Is the patient stable or unstable? Does it look narrow or wide? Is it regular? If narrow + regular + stable, go straight to Valsalva + adenosine. If any doubt about VT or pre‑excitation, escalate. Most SVT is benign and curable – but the miss is always catastrophic.

## Supraventricular Tachycardia
**One-liner:** A narrow‑complex tachycardia (≥150 bpm) originating above the ventricles, common in young adults, that usually terminates with vagal manoeuvres or adenosine – misdiagnosis or missing pre‑excited AF can be dangerous.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
- **Classic vignette:** A 28‑year‑old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden‑onset palpitations, light‑headedness, and a sense of impending doom.   - *Young adult + female* → AVNRT is more common in women; WPW often presents in childhood/young adulthood.
  - *Sense of impending doom + light‑headedness* → reduced cerebral perfusion due to high rate and short diastole; patient may also have polyuria (ANP release).
  - *Narrow QRS* → supraventricular origin; rate >150 rules out sinus tachycardia in most cases.
  - *No visible P waves* → in AVNRT, atria and ventricles are activated simultaneously so P waves are buried in the QRS; in AVRT, P waves may be seen after the QRS (retrograde P).
  - *Adenosine response* → termination of tachycardia + brief sinus pause is diagnostic of AVNRT/AVRT (adenosine‑sensitive circuit). Atrial flutter will not terminate – it may slow transiently.
- **Atypical presentations:**
  - **Wide QRS SVT (aberrancy):** Pre‑existing bundle branch block or rate‑dependent aberrancy mimics ventricular tachycardia (VT). Clues: history of SVT, adenosine termination, concordance of QRS morphology with known baseline BBB.
  - **Pre‑excited atrial fibrillation (WPW + AF):** Very fast irregular wide‑complex tachycardia (may exceed 250–300 bpm) – no adenosine! Risk of degeneration to VF. Look for delta waves in sinus rhythm.
  - **SVT in elderly:** May present with syncope, falls, or heart failure (rate‑related cardiomyopathy) without palpitations. ECG may show atrial flutter with variable block.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
- Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) is any tachyarrhythmia originating above the bifurcation of the His bundle, with a narrow QRS (<120 ms) unless conduction is aberrant. The most common types are AVNRT (~60%), AVRT (~30%), and atrial tachycardia (~10%).
- It matters because SVT affects 2.25 per 1000 people, often causing debilitating symptoms and emergency visits. Most patients have structurally normal hearts, so management is safe and curative. Pitfalls: confusing SVT with VT (leads to wrong treatment), giving adenosine in pre‑excited AF (risk of converting to VF), or missing tachyarrhythmia‑induced cardiomyopathy.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
- **The circuit story:** In AVNRT, there are two functional pathways inside the AV node – a "slow" pathway with a short refractory period and a "fast" pathway with a long refractory period. A premature atrial contraction (PAC) blocks in the fast pathway (still refractory) but conducts down the slow pathway. By the time the impulse reaches the AV node exit, the fast pathway has recovered, allowing the impulse to travel back up (retrograde) – creating a re‑entry loop. The atria and ventricles are activated nearly simultaneously, so P waves are hidden.
- **Why the heart rate is rapid:** The re‑entrant circuit has a short cycle length (typically 160–250 ms), so the ventricles are captured at every beat (1:1 conduction) giving rates of 150–250 bpm.
- **Why patients feel light‑headed:** High rate reduces diastolic filling time → decreased stroke volume → drop in cerebral perfusion. The sudden onset also triggers a catecholamine surge (anxiety, pallor).
- **Why vagal manoeuvres/adenosine work:** They increase AV nodal refractoriness, interrupting the re‑entrant loop. If the circuit is not AV node‑dependent (e.g., atrial tachycardia) they only slow the ventricular rate but don't terminate it.
- **Why the ECG shows no P waves (AVNRT):** Atrial activation is retrograde and simultaneous with ventricular activation – P waves are buried in the QRS or appear as “pseudo‑r’ in V1. In typical AVRT (WPW), the circuit goes down the AV node and up the accessory pathway, so the atria activate slightly after the ventricles → P waves seen just after the QRS (retrograde P, often inverted in II/III/aVF).

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | AVNRT (no structural heart disease) | Young, female, recurrent episodes since teens |
| **Congenital** | Accessory pathway (WPW) | Delta waves on sinus ECG, sudden onset in children |
| **Reversible triggers** | Caffeine, alcohol, stress, thyroid storm, electrolyte abnormalities (hypomagnesemia, hypokalemia) | Patient reports heavy coffee intake or exam stress |
| **Drug‑induced** | Sympathomimetics, theophylline, digoxin toxicity (atrial tachycardia with block) | Patient on inhalers or heart failure meds |
| **Cardiac pathology** | Valvular disease, cardiomyopathy, pulmonary embolism (atrial flutter/fibrillation) | Dyspnoea, raised JVP, murmur, acute hypoxia |
| **Post‑operative** | Cardiac surgery (atrial incisions/scars → atrial flutter/tachycardia) | Recent heart surgery, ICU patient |

- **Most common cause overall:** AV nodal re‑entrant tachycardia (AVNRT) in the absence of structural disease.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
- **12‑lead ECG during tachycardia – gold standard:**
  - *What to look for:* Narrow QRS (<120 ms), rate >150, regularity, P‑wave morphology/location.
  - *Confirmation of SVT:* Terminates with adenosine (diagnostic for AVNRT/AVRT).
  - *Pattern recognition:* If visible P waves are inverted in II/III/aVF and appear before the QRS → atrial tachycardia (rate usually 100–250 bpm). If they appear after the QRS (ST‑segment) → AVRT (retrograde P).

  - *What rules out VT:* QRS duration ≤120 ms; if wide, look for dissociated P waves or fusion beats (VT). In pre‑excited AF, wide QRS but irregularly irregular – cannot give adenosine.
- **ECG in sinus rhythm (baseline):**
  - Look for delta waves (WPW) or short PR interval (Lown‑Ganong‑Levine syndrome).
  - Normal ECG does not rule out AVNRT.
- **Echocardiogram:**
  - *Why:* To exclude structural heart disease, assess LV function (rate‑related cardiomyopathy).
  - *When:* All first‑presentation SVT, especially if response to adenosine is atypical or history of syncope.
- **Holter monitor (24–48 h) or event recorder:**

  - *Why:* Rule out reversible causes; high TSH low T4 suggests thyrotoxicosis; K <3.5 or Mg <0.7 augments re‑entry.

  - *Why:* Definitive diagnosis + catheter ablation.
  - *When:* Recurrent symptomatic SVT, failed medical therapy, WPW with pre‑excited AF, high‑risk occupations (pilots, athletes).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
- **Immediate (first hour) – assume haemodynamically stable:**
  1. **Vagal manoeuvres** – Valsalva or carotid sinus massage (in absence of carotid bruit). *Why:* Increases vagal tone, blocks AV node, terminates re‑entrant circuit. Success rate ~30–50%.
  2. **Adenosine** – 6 mg rapid IV push (≥2 second) followed by 10 mL saline flush, then if no effect at 1–2 min give 12 mg, max 12 mg.
     - *Why:* Blocks AV nodal conduction – terminates AVNRT/AVRT.
     - *What to watch:* Transient asystole, flushing, bronchospasm (avoid in asthma – use verapamil instead).
     - *Pitfall:* If rhythm is atrial flutter/tachycardia, you'll see transient slowing but no termination; do not repeat unnecessarily.
  3. **If failed:** Synchronised cardioversion (50–100 J biphasic) if unstable (hypotension, chest pain, altered mental state – rare in pure SVT). *Why:* Direct current interrupts re‑entry.
- **Ward (24–72 h) – after first episode:**
  - **Monitor rhythm** (telemetry for 24 h) to detect recurrence.
  - **Start long‑term rate control** if recurrence is likely or patient wishes:
    - First‑line (AVNRT/AVRT): β‑blocker (e.g., metoprolol 25–50 mg PO BD) or verapamil 40–120 mg PO TDS.
    - *Why:* Slows AV nodal conduction and reduces triggers.
  - **WPW patients:** Risk of pre‑excited AF – avoid verapamil/digoxin (may enhance accessory pathway conduction). Flecainide 50–150 mg PO BD is preferred (class IC) – but contraindicated in structural heart disease.
  - **Patient education:** Advise to avoid triggers (caffeine, alcohol), demonstrate Valsalva, and return if palpitations recur.
- **Long‑term:**
  - **Catheter ablation** – definitive curative procedure (success >95% for AVNRT/AVRT). *Why:* Eliminates the re‑entrant circuit.
  - **When:** Recurrent SVT despite medication, WPW with high risk (pre‑excited AF, syncope), drug intolerance, or patient preference.
  - **After ablation:** Recurrence ~5%; lifelong follow‑up with ECG if WPW ablated (delta wave may persist).
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do not use adenosine in wide‑complex tachycardia of uncertain origin (may cause VF if VT).
  - Do not give verapamil/digoxin in WPW + AF – they slow AV node conduction but can accelerate conduction through accessory pathway, risking VF.
  - Do not delay cardioversion in unstable patient – adenosine is not safe when BP is low.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | SVT (AVNRT/AVRT) | Atrial Flutter | Atrial Fibrillation | Ventricular Tachycardia |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| **Rate** | 150–250 | 150 (if 2:1) or 300 (1:1) | 100–180 (uncontrolled) | 100–250 |
| **Rhythm** | Regular | Regular (sawtooth F waves) | Irregularly irregular | Usually regular |
| **Onset/Offset** | Abrupt | Can be abrupt or gradual | Often gradual | Usually abrupt |
| **QRS width** | Narrow (<120 ms) | Narrow (unless wide with block) | Narrow (unless pre‑excited) | Wide (>120 ms) |
| **P waves** | Hidden (AVNRT) or after QRS (AVRT) | Flutter waves (sawtooth in II/III/aVF) | Fibrillatory waves, no P | Dissociated P waves (AV dissociation) |
| **Response to adenosine** | Termination | No termination – transient slowing | No termination – transient slowing | No change (rarely termination) |
| **Key differentiator** | Adenosine terminates | Flutter waves + carotid massage slows rate | Irregular R‑R intervals | AV dissociation, fusion beats |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
- **Tachyarrhythmia‑induced cardiomyopathy:** Chronic uncontrolled SVT (rates >130 for months) can cause reversible left ventricular systolic dysfunction. *Presentation:* Exertional dyspnoea, fatigue, signs of heart failure. *Key signs:* Tachycardia on ECG, low LVEF on echo. *What to do:* Rate control or ablation → LVEF often normalises over 3–6 months.
- **Pre‑excited AF → VF:** Missed WPW + AF → extremely fast ventricular response (250+ bpm) can degenerate into VF/sudden cardiac death. *Presentation:* Collapse, wide irregular tachycardia. *Management:* Immediate DC cardioversion (200 J). *Pitfall:* Do not give AV nodal blockers (adenosine, verapamil, beta blockers) – they may increase accessory pathway conduction.
- **Syncope/falls:** Severe cerebral hypoperfusion during prolonged episode → injury. *Late presentation:* Elderly hip fracture from fall during palpitations.
- **Cardiogenic shock (rare):** Prolonged high‑rate SVT with structural heart disease → pulmonary oedema. *Signs:* Raised JVP, basal crackles, hypotension. *Action:* Urgent synchronised cardioversion.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
- **Mnemonic for SVT causes in the young:** “AVN” (AVNRT, AVRT – WPW, Atrial Tachycardia).
- **Mnemonic for distinguishing SVT from VT:** "Brugada criteria" – but simpler: **HAS** (Haemodynamics, Adenosine response, Scar?) – if haemodynamically unstable → assume VT until proven. If adenosine terminates → SVT.
- **Pearls:**
  1. **Always get a 12‑lead during tachycardia** – not just in sinus rhythm.
  2. **If you see a wide‑complex tachycardia and the rhythm is irregular, think pre‑excited AF first** – do NOT give adenosine.
  3. **Valsalva is underused** – instruct patient to blow into a 10 mL syringe (to pop the plunger out) while supine; increased success.
  4. **Adenosine has a diagnostic window** – even if it doesn't terminate, transient slowing reveals underlying atrial activity (flutter waves).

  6. **Children with WPW and syncope** – risk stratification: if pre‑excitation persists during exercise stress test, ablation is indicated.
  7. **Never discharge a patient with SVT without a baseline ECG** – if delta waves are present, avoid verapamil.
- **Common exam/ward mistakes:**
  - Calling tachycardia “sinus” without checking rate >150 (sinus rarely >150 in adults).
  - Giving adenosine in AF with WPW.
  - Mistaking atrial flutter with 2:1 block for sinus tachycardia – the flutter waves are often hidden; check for sawtooth in II, III, aVF.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU team for:**
  - Haemodynamic instability: SBP <90 mmHg, altered mental state, chest pain, acute pulmonary oedema.
  - Wide‑complex tachycardia of uncertain origin (especially irregular) – needs cardiologist input urgently.
  - SVT refractory to adenosine (two doses) with ongoing symptoms – consider elective cardioversion.
  - Pre‑excited AF (WPW) – high risk of VF; manage in a monitored area with defibrillator nearby.
  - Recurrent SVT despite medical therapy in a patient with structural heart disease (low EF) – risk of decompensation.
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥7** (any single parameter red: RR >25, SpO2 <91%, SBP <91, HR >131) → escalate immediately.
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (indicating tissue hypoperfusion) → consider cardioversion.
  - **GCS ≤13** (especially if decreased from baseline) → protect airway, call for anaesthetic support.
  - **Sustained rate >250 bpm** → risk of degeneration; prepare defibrillator.
  - **Ventricular rate >200 bpm in AF** → risk of pre‑excitation; cardiology review.

*Final thought:* In any tachyarrhythmia, take a step back: Is the patient stable or unstable? Does it look narrow or wide? Is it regular? If narrow + regular + stable, go straight to Valsalva + adenosine. If any doubt about VT or pre‑excitation, escalate. Most SVT is benign and curable – but the miss is always catastrophic.
